---
title: "When Should a Computer Work?"
subtitle: "The same computation, run four hours apart, can carry half the carbon. A data story on carbon-aware computing — draft skeleton."
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd
from pathlib import Path

processed = Path("../data/processed")
ci = pd.read_parquet(processed / "uk_ci_hourly.parquet")["ci_actual"]
sim = pd.read_parquet(processed / "uk_sim_daily.parquet")
totals = sim.sum()
saving = 100 * (1 - totals["forecast"] / totals["naive"])
```

*(Draft structure — prose placeholders; German data pending. Numbers below are live from the UK pipeline.)*

## The grid breathes

Electricity is not equally clean around the clock. Every kilowatt-hour you draw
at 8pm carries more CO₂ than one drawn at noon, because the mix of power plants
behind it changes hour by hour.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Mean grid carbon intensity by hour of day and month, Great Britain 2024–2025."
import plotly.express as px

heat = ci.groupby([ci.index.month, ci.index.hour]).mean().unstack(0)
fig = px.imshow(
    heat.T,
    labels=dict(x="hour of day", y="month", color="gCO₂/kWh"),
    color_continuous_scale="RdYlGn_r",
    aspect="auto",
)
fig.update_layout(margin=dict(l=0, r=0, t=10, b=0))
fig.show()
```

## The experiment

One four-hour training job per day, free to run at any hour. Scheduled by our
day-ahead forecast instead of at a random time, over a year it emitted
**`{python} f"{saving:.0f}%"` less CO₂** — with perfect foresight the ceiling is
`{python} f"{100 * (1 - totals['oracle'] / totals['naive']):.0f}%"`.

*(Full narrative, the drag-the-job interactive, and the German story to come.)*
